mappings.rationaleAI: Foundational chemistry covering energy transfer and entropy in reactions (B, B.3), general principles of chemical transformation and conservation (A, A.1), and reaction mechanisms as part of core chemical understanding (D).

Thermodynamics including entropy and free energy changes in reactions (B, B.3), activation energy and reaction kinetics (B.2, C), temperature effects on reaction rates (C.1.b), and synthesis processes relating to reaction mechanisms (D).

Conservation of energy as the first law relates to conservation of mass and energy in reactions (A.1), entropy and the second law governing the directionality of chemical processes (B.3), classification of reactions by energy changes as exothermic or endothermic (B.1), and free energy determining chemical equilibrium (B.3).
Entropy as a measure of disorder and ignorance directly relevant to free energy and entropy changes in chemical reactions and the thermodynamic relations governing chemical equilibrium (B.3).

Reaction mechanisms for organic compounds including substitution, addition, and elimination reactions (A.3.a.iv, A.3.a.v, D.5), classification of organic reactions by mechanism type (D.4), bonding theory and functional groups governing reaction pathways (D), and synthetic techniques and choice of synthetic paths (H, H.1).

Chemical bonding including covalent and hydrogen bonds underlying reaction behavior (A), and molecular recognition and interaction mechanisms as foundations for understanding how reactions proceed at the molecular level (D).

Historical development from ancient element theory through Lavoisier's chemical nomenclature relating to growth of theories concerning chemical reactions (A.1), and oxidation-reduction concepts in the context of elemental chemistry (F).

Development of atomic theory and electron configuration underlying the growth of major theories concerning chemical reactions and the relationship between elemental properties and chemical reactivity (A.2).

Lavoisier's revolution in understanding chemical change and the historical development of reaction theory (A.2), and the transforming power of chemical synthesis tracing the evolution of synthetic chemistry (H).

Biological catalysts accelerating reactions by many orders of magnitude directly relevant to catalysis affecting reaction rates (C.1.d), factors influencing the course of enzymatic reactions including transition states and catalysts (D.1), and oxidation-reduction reactions in biological systems involving enzymatic processes (F.5).
Enzymatic processes and metabolic pathways involving catalysis of biochemical reactions (C.1.d), and oxidation-reduction reactions in biological systems through cellular metabolic chemistry (F.5).
Physics of light including electromagnetic radiation directly relevant to photochemical processes (G.1) and the role of light in initiating and driving chemical reactions (G).
Chemistry of combustion as an oxidation-reduction reaction in flames (F.6), and the exothermic nature of fire as classification of reactions according to energy changes (B.1).

Molecular structure of water governing its role as a solvent and reactant influencing the course of reactions (D.1), and water's acid-base chemistry including hydrogen bonding relevant to acid-base reactions and equilibria (E).
Isotopic substitution effects on reaction rates and mechanisms, directly relevant to how variant forms of elements with different neutron numbers affect chemical kinetics (C.1.g).

Quantum mechanical framework providing theoretical basis for understanding electron behavior in chemical reactions (A.2) and the quantum foundations of reaction mechanisms including transition states (D).
Mass spectrometry and synchrotron radiation as experimental methods applicable to studying chemical kinetics and reaction products (C.5).

Chemical bonding and fabrication processes connecting material properties to chemical transformations, relevant to basic concepts of chemical reactions involving conservation and transformation (A.1).
Polymers and colloids relevant to polymerization reactions as a principal kind of chemical reaction (A.3.a.ii).
